inputs.story: Julia has hired several new people to work as ride-managers for Walton World Amusement Park. She needs to check on their results after their first full day at work. Help her match each new employee to their ride and section of the park (broken down by color), as well as the number of people each served during that day. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The worker who works in the green section served 25 more riders than Andy.
2. Of the employee who works in the red section and the worker who works in the yellow section, one served 50 riders and the other is Victor.
3. The employee who works in the green section is either Zachary or the worker who served 100 riders.
4. The person who works in the yellow section served 50 more riders than Zachary.
5. Victor served more riders than the worker who works in the blue section.
inputs.constants:
employees: Andy; Brett; Victor; Zachary.
riders: 50 riders; 75 riders; 100 riders; 125 riders.
section: blue; green; red; yellow.
inputs.solution:
Riders	Employees	Section
50 riders	Andy	red
75 riders	Zachary	green
100 riders	Brett	blue
125 riders	Victor	yellow

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Julia has hired several new people to work as ride-managers for Walton World Amusement Park. She needs to check on their results after their first full day at work. Help her match each new employee to their ride and section of the park (broken down by color), as well as the number of people each served during that day. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The worker who works in the green section served 25 more riders than Andy.
2. Of the employee who works in the red section and the worker who works in the yellow section, one served 50 riders and the other is Victor.
3. The employee who works in the green section is either Zachary or the worker who served 100 riders.
4. The person who works in the yellow section served 50 more riders than Zachary.
5. Victor served more riders than the worker who works in the blue section.

Constants:
employees: Andy; Brett; Victor; Zachary.
riders: 50 riders; 75 riders; 100 riders; 125 riders.
section: blue; green; red; yellow.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

Each category has 4 distinct constants.

riders: 50, 75, 100, 125
section: blue, green, red, yellow

riders(50; 75; 100; 125).

Search space:
{assign(E, S, R): section(S), riders(R)}=1 :- employee(E).

Uniqueness:
{E1=E2; S1=S2; R1=R2}=0 :- assign(E1,S1,R1), assign(E2,S2,R2), (E1,S1,R1)!=(E2,S2,R2).

Clues:
1. The worker who works in the green section served 25 more riders than Andy.
   Let E_g be the employee in green section with riders R_g.
   R_g = R_a + 25, where R_a is Andy's riders.
   Constraint: {R = R2 + 25} = 1 :- assign(E1, "green", R), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Andy".

2. Of the employee who works in the red section and the worker who works in the yellow section, one served 50 riders and the other is Victor.
   This means:
   - The red section worker and yellow section worker are different.
   - One has riders=50, the other is Victor.
   So:
   C1 != C2 :- assign(C1, "red", _), assign(C2, "yellow", _).
   {R=50; E="Victor"}=1 :- assign(E, "red", R).
   {R=50; E="Victor"}=1 :- assign(E, "yellow", R).

3. The employee who works in the green section is either Zachary or the worker who served 100 riders.
   {E="Zachary"; R=100}=1 :- assign(E, "green", R).

4. The person who works in the yellow section served 50 more riders than Zachary.
   R_y = R_z + 50.
   {R = R2 + 50} = 1 :- assign(E1, "yellow", R), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Zachary".

5. Victor served more riders than the worker who works in the blue section.
   R_v > R_b.
   :- assign(E1, _, R1), assign(E2, "blue", R2), E1="Victor", R1 <= R2.
   Or: R1 > R2 :- assign(E1, _, R1), assign(E2, "blue", R2), E1="Victor".

Let's verify clues and translate them carefully.

Clue 1: "The worker who works in the green section served 25 more riders than Andy."
=> R(green) = R(Andy) + 25.
ASP: {R1 = R2 + 25} = 1 :- assign(E1, "green", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Andy".

Clue 2: "Of the employee who works in the red section and the worker who works in the yellow section, one served 50 riders and the other is Victor."
This implies:
- Red section employee != Yellow section employee.
- Red section employee has R=50 OR is Victor.
- Yellow section employee has R=50 OR is Victor.
- Exactly one of them has R=50, and exactly one is Victor. (Since they are different people, one is Victor (R!=50 presumably, unless Victor served 50, but if Victor served 50, then both would be Victor and 50, which contradicts they are different. So Victor's riders != 50).
Actually, the standard logic grid translation for "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" is:
A != B.
{A is C; A is D} = 1.
{B is C; B is D} = 1.
Wait, if A is C (R=50) and B is D (E=Victor), or A is D and B is C.
So:
assign(Red_E, "red", R_red).
assign(Yel_E, "yellow", R_yel).
Red_E != Yel_E.
{R_red = 50; Red_E = "Victor"} = 1.
{R_yel = 50; Yel_E = "Victor"} = 1.
This covers it.

Clue 3: "The employee who works in the green section is either Zachary or the worker who served 100 riders."
{E = "Zachary"; R = 100} = 1 :- assign(E, "green", R).

Clue 4: "The person who works in the yellow section served 50 more riders than Zachary."
R(yellow) = R(Zachary) + 50.

R(Victor) > R(blue).

Differences: 25, 50, 75.
Clue 1: R(green) = R(Andy) + 25. Possible pairs: (50,75), (75,100), (100,125).
Clue 4: R(yellow) = R(Zachary) + 50. Possible pairs: (50,100), (75,125).

Let's test consistency mentally:
If R(yellow) = 100, R(Zachary) = 50.

Clue 3: Green is Zachary or R=100.
If R(yellow)=100, then Green != Yellow, so Green is Zachary. Then R(green) = R(Zachary) = 50. But R(green) = R(Andy)+25 => R(Andy)=25, not in list. So R(yellow) != 100.

Andy: riders=50.
Yellow: riders=125.
Red/Yellow from clue 2: One is Victor (riders!=50), one is 50 riders.
Yellow has 125 riders, so Yellow is NOT 50 riders. Thus Yellow is Victor.
So Victor: section=yellow, riders=125.
Then Red must be the one with 50 riders.

Who is Red? Andy has 50 riders. So Andy: section=red, riders=50.
Blue section: remaining section. Brett must be blue.
Victor (125) > Blue (Brett). Brett's riders must be 100.
Check: Blue riders = 100.
Remaining: Brett: section=blue, riders=100.
All consistent!
Solution:
Andy: red, 50
Brett: blue, 100
Victor: yellow, 125
Zachary: green, 75

Let's assemble the program.

```asp
% Constants
employee("Andy"; "Brett"; "Victor"; "Zachary").
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "red"; "yellow").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one section S and riders R.
{assign(E, S, R): section(S), riders(R)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; S1=S2; R1=R2}=0 :- assign(E1,S1,R1), assign(E2,S2,R2), (E1,S1,R1)!=(E2,S2,R2).

% 1. The worker who works in the green section served 25 more riders than Andy.
{R1 = R2 + 25} = 1 :- assign(E1, "green", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Andy".

% 2. Of the employee who works in the red section and the worker who works in the yellow section, one served 50 riders and the other is Victor.
:- assign(E1, "red", _), assign(E2, "yellow", _), E1 = E2.
{R1 = 50; E1 = "Victor"} = 1 :- assign(E1, "red", R1).
{R2 = 50; E2 = "Victor"} = 1 :- assign(E2, "yellow", R2).

% 3. The employee who works in the green section is either Zachary or the worker who served 100 riders.
{E = "Zachary"; R = 100} = 1 :- assign(E, "green", R).

% 4. The person who works in the yellow section served 50 more riders than Zachary.
{R1 = R2 + 50} = 1 :- assign(E1, "yellow", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Zachary".

% 5. Victor served more riders than the worker who works in the blue section.
R1 > R2 :- assign(E1, _, R1), assign(E2, "blue", R2), E1="Victor".

#show assign/3.
```

Does it imply exactly one served 50 and exactly one is Victor? Yes.
My rules:
`{R1 = 50; E1 = "Victor"} = 1 :- assign(E1, "red", R1).`
`{R2 = 50; E2 = "Victor"} = 1 :- assign(E2, "yellow", R2).`
This ensures each satisfies the condition. Since they are different employees, one will be Victor (with R != 50) and the other will have R=50 (with E != Victor). This correctly captures the clue.

Check Clue 5: `R1 > R2 :- assign(E1, _, R1), assign(E2, "blue", R2), E1="Victor".`
This is a valid rule. It forces the head to be true if body is true. Since riders are integers, this works.

One detail: Clingo's `{Head} = 1` syntax requires that the head is a disjunction or a single atom. `R1 = R2 + 25` is a constraint on values, but in Clingo, `R1 = R2 + 25` in a head with `{...}=1` is actually a choice rule that selects exactly one combination of values satisfying the equation? Wait, no.
In Clingo, `{R1 = R2 + 25} = 1` means that for each assignment to E1, E2, exactly one pair (R1, R2) from the domain satisfies the equation? Actually, Clingo's `=` in head represents a constraint on the values, but it's safer to write it as a constraint or use `:- not (R1 = R2 + 25) :- ...`.
However, the example 1 uses:
`P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".`
So they use `:-` for constraints or `P1=P2-50 :- ...` as a rule. I will follow the example style: `R1 = R2 + 25 :- assign(E1, "green", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Andy".`
Wait, example 1 uses:
`P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".`
This is a rule, not a choice rule. It forces P1 to be P2-50.
But if we use `R1 = R2 + 25 :- ...`, Clingo might complain because R1 and R2 are variables in the body that are already bound by `assign/3`. Actually, `assign(E1, "green", R1)` binds R1. `assign(E2, _, R2)` binds R2. So `R1 = R2 + 25` in the head is a constraint that checks if the bound values satisfy the equation. If not, the rule fails (or rather, it's a constraint: `:- assign(...), not (R1 = R2 + 25).`).
To be safe and follow Clingo conventions, I'll use constraints:
`:- assign(E1, "green", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Andy", not (R1 = R2 + 25).`
Or simply:
`R1 = R2 + 25 :- assign(E1, "green", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Andy".`
In Clingo, `Head :- Body.` means if Body is true, Head must be true. Since R1 and R2 are already instantiated by the body, `R1 = R2 + 25` acts as a filter. It's standard in these puzzles. I'll stick to `R1 = R2 + 25 :- ...` as in Example 1.

Wait, Example 1 says:
`P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".`
This is exactly what I'll use.

Let's verify Clue 1 with this format:

asp_generation.response:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Andy"; "Brett"; "Victor"; "Zachary").
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "red"; "yellow").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one section S and riders R.
{assign(E, S, R): section(S), riders(R)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; S1=S2; R1=R2}=0 :- assign(E1,S1,R1), assign(E2,S2,R2), (E1,S1,R1)!=(E2,S2,R2).

% 1. The worker who works in the green section served 25 more riders than Andy.
R1 = R2 + 25 :- assign(E1, "green", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Andy".

% 2. Of the employee who works in the red section and the worker who works in the yellow section, one served 50 riders and the other is Victor.
:- assign(E1, "red", _), assign(E2, "yellow", _), E1 = E2.
{R1 = 50; E1 = "Victor"} = 1 :- assign(E1, "red", R1).
{R2 = 50; E2 = "Victor"} = 1 :- assign(E2, "yellow", R2).

% 3. The employee who works in the green section is either Zachary or the worker who served 100 riders.
{E = "Zachary"; R = 100} = 1 :- assign(E, "green", R).

% 4. The person who works in the yellow section served 50 more riders than Zachary.
R1 = R2 + 50 :- assign(E1, "yellow", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Zachary".

% 5. Victor served more riders than the worker who works in the blue section.
R1 > R2 :- assign(E1, _, R1), assign(E2, "blue", R2), E1="Victor".

#show assign/3.
```

% Constants
employee("Andy"; "Brett"; "Victor"; "Zachary").
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "red"; "yellow").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one section S and riders R.
{assign(E, S, R): section(S), riders(R)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; S1=S2; R1=R2}=0 :- assign(E1,S1,R1), assign(E2,S2,R2), (E1,S1,R1)!=(E2,S2,R2).

% 1. The worker who works in the green section served 25 more riders than Andy.
R1 = R2 + 25 :- assign(E1, "green", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Andy".

% 2. Of the employee who works in the red section and the worker who works in the yellow section, one served 50 riders and the other is Victor.
:- assign(E1, "red", _), assign(E2, "yellow", _), E1 = E2.
{R1 = 50; E1 = "Victor"} = 1 :- assign(E1, "red", R1).
{R2 = 50; E2 = "Victor"} = 1 :- assign(E2, "yellow", R2).

% 3. The employee who works in the green section is either Zachary or the worker who served 100 riders.
{E = "Zachary"; R = 100} = 1 :- assign(E, "green", R).

% 4. The person who works in the yellow section served 50 more riders than Zachary.
R1 = R2 + 50 :- assign(E1, "yellow", R1), assign(E2, _, R2), E2="Zachary".

% 5. Victor served more riders than the worker who works in the blue section.
R1 > R2 :- assign(E1, _, R1), assign(E2, "blue", R2), E1="Victor".

#show assign/3.
